welcome.headline:
Motivation is not
a feeling.
welcome.headlineSub:
One action per day, honest check-in,
reconnect when you fall.

0.title: One clear action
0.sub: Zero decision fatigue every day — the card tells you what to do.

1.title: Anchor system
1.sub: Attach to an existing routine; no need to carve out new time.

2.title: Built for recovery
2.sub: Never punishes you for falling. Helps you reconnect.

step1.title:
Who do you want
to be in 66 days?
step1.sub: Pick a direction — you'll attach an anchor and time next.
step1.infoBody: No exact match needed. Pick the closest card; refine it with your anchor and habit name.
step1.customBlurb: Write a custom line; set anchor and time in the next step.
step1.customPlaceholder: E.g. Someone who moves daily and takes care of themselves…

step2.title:
Where will you
anchor this identity?
step2.sub: Start after something you already do — brains love an «after this» cue.
step2.commitmentPlaceholder: Your commitment sentence appears when you pick an anchor…

step3.title:
Why did you want
this identity?

promises.0: Suggests 1 action daily — zero thinking, zero decision fatigue.

plans.emptyTodayBody: The list you wrote in Journey yesterday appears here. You can set the first plan for tomorrow.
plans.tomorrowHint: Add 1 main + max 2 support items for a clear anchor tomorrow morning.
plans.planHint: Check off or confirm the day with check-in.
plans.planHintShort: Tap to check off · lock the day with check-in

day1.title: The process continues
day1.body: Missing 1 day is about 1.5% of the 66-day path. The neural pathway is open — a small repeat in the same direction with today's card is enough.

day2.title: Time to scale down
day2.body: Skipping two days in a row points to an over-ambitious goal. Scale the behaviour to 2 minutes, keep the same anchor; choose the small version on today's card and do an honest check-in.

day3.title: Reconnect
day3.body: You've disconnected for a few days — this doesn't mean your brain forgot the path. Lally's curve varies widely between individuals: scale down, refresh the trigger/anchor; choose the smallest clear step from today's card and restart with a real check-in.

actionGate.strictBody: Complete today's suggested step before confirming the day. It's short — for momentum.
actionGate.softBody: Check-in builds the habit; but a 30-second move genuinely primes the brain.
actionGate.strictHint: Strict mode on — you can turn it off in Profile → Advanced preferences.

firstWeek.title: First week — small map
firstWeek.body: The app is dense; these five small steps settle the initial feel.
firstWeek.stepCheckIn: Check-in or action from Today's box
firstWeek.stepAction: At least one live action (from card)
firstWeek.stepMind: Write even one line in the Mind tab
firstWeek.stepTomorrow: Write a main item for tomorrow in Journey

1.principle: Starting is hundreds of times more important than perfecting.
1.science: Started tasks stay open in the mind (Zeigarnik) — the urge to close pulls you back.
1.action: Shrink the habit to today's smallest version; start just once.

2.principle: Motivation is a feeling; feelings pass, your system and anchor remain.
2.science: Self-Determination Theory: sustainable behavior needs autonomy and meaning (Deci & Ryan).
2.action: Write your why in 10 seconds, then start only at the trigger moment.

3.principle: The brain learns through repetition, not through feeling perfect.
3.science: Hebb's rule: neurons that fire together wire together — each anchor rep strengthens the path.
3.action: Repeat the same anchor at the same time once more.

4.principle: Small behavior quietly beats big resistance.
4.science: Implementation intentions make planned trigger-action pairs far more reliable (Gollwitzer).
4.action: Say your if-then aloud: 'When X happens, I will do Y.'

5.principle: Distraction is instinct; the answer is designing your environment.
5.science: Less friction means less difficulty — behavior design beats willpower (Thaler & Sunstein).
5.action: Prep tools or setup tonight in 30 seconds.

6.principle: Identity starts as a sentence, then grows through repeated action.
6.science: Self-schema: a consistent behavior story reinforces identity (BEM / identity reflection).
6.action: Say today: 'I am someone who starts' — then do one rep.

7.principle: Seven days is a strong first signal for the brain to open a new path.
7.science: Synaptic plasticity strengthens with repetition; duration varies by person (Lally).
7.action: Return to day-one's mini version and complete one more round.

8.principle: The urge to quit is the old path defending itself — normal and temporary.
8.science: Old habit circuits in the basal ganglia compete with thin new pathways (Graybiel; Yin & Knowlton).
8.action: Say one win from yesterday aloud — positive detail feeds the new path.

9.principle: Sometimes the best reward is simply the 'I finished' feeling.
9.science: Small completion rewards reinforce dopamine balance; excess weakens intrinsic drive.
9.action: Give yourself a tiny, healthy 'marker reward' right after finishing.

10.principle: Consistency is more reliable than intensity.
10.science: Starting threshold: small reps are enough for growth; skipping breaks motivation.
10.action: Focus on 'did I show up today?' not duration or volume.

11.principle: Even one rep saves the day when the mind wanders.
11.science: When cognitive load is high, procedural memory is suppressed — mini version lowers load.
11.action: Choose today's smallest version; presence before perfection.

12.principle: Same anchor, same time — the pattern starts to be recognized.
12.science: Repetition strengthens procedural learning; 21 days isn't magic, it's signal accumulation (Lally).
12.action: Don't change the anchor; start once more at the same point.

13.principle: One step from two weeks — patience beats speed.
13.science: The automaticity curve is slow and wavy — boredom is part of the process (Lally, 2009).
13.action: Just be visible today; mark presence, not duration.

14.principle: Fourteen days: you're leaving a daily trace, not running a trial.
14.science: Self-monitoring and tracking meaningfully improve behavior (Harkin meta-analysis).
14.action: Mark today's rep — data is your progress map.

15.principle: When you tell someone, you stop going it alone.
15.science: Support or an audience can gently boost motivation on medium tasks (Zajonc).
15.action: Tell one person your intention in a single sentence today.

16.principle: Without a set time, the day slips away.
16.science: Planning when you'll act roughly doubles follow-through (Gollwitzer).
16.action: Block 5 minutes on your calendar — turn on a reminder or ask someone to nudge you.

17.principle: Procrastination isn't laziness — sometimes it's your brain saying it's tired.
17.science: Being hard on yourself prolongs delay; a gentle start works better (Neff).
17.action: Take one 30-second step before judging yourself.

18.principle: When things are vague, your brain picks delay — a clear plan saves you.
18.science: If-then plans steer impulse responses on autopilot (Gollwitzer).
18.action: Rewrite today's trigger in one line: 'When X, I'll Y.'

19.principle: Boredom means you're entering the tunnel toward automation.
19.science: Automaticity comes slowly and unevenly; boredom is a normal part (Lally).
19.action: Do the same rep — just shift the setting slightly (different room, same move).

20.principle: Limits don't shrink freedom; they lighten decision load.
20.science: Fewer choices cut mental fatigue and make sticking easier (Baumeister).
20.action: Set today's ceiling in 2 minutes — know when you're done, not how long you lasted.

21.principle: Week three: 'I'm doing this' is no longer just an intention.
21.science: Repetition strengthens procedural learning — 21 days isn't magic, it's signal stacking (Lally).
21.action: Write a real number for how many reps you've done so far.

22.principle: Setup is done: winning now means staying small, not scaling up.
22.science: Motivation wobbles at phase transitions; protecting cues beats pushing intensity (Lally; Wood).
22.action: Promise tomorrow stays at day-one mini size or smaller.

23.principle: Motivation will dip — that's the plan, not a breakdown.
23.science: The automaticity curve is slow and bumpy; dips are normal (Lally, 2009).
23.action: Don't measure motivation; protect today's single rep.

24.principle: Identity starts as a sentence, then grows with small daily proof.
24.science: Behavior updates self-concept — words and action work together (self-schema).
24.action: Grow your 'I'm someone who…' line one step; keep the move tiny.

25.principle: What's visible keeps going; what hides fades.
25.action: Leave one-line proof for today: date plus a small mark.

26.principle: Resisting resistance trains your stop muscle — but rest matters too.
26.science: Inhibition control works but depletes; recovery is required (Baumeister).
26.action: One deep breath at the trigger, then one rep — skip the debate.

27.principle: Give credit to today's you, not yesterday's.
27.science: Feeling capable feeds intrinsic drive — notice small progress (Deci & Ryan).
27.action: Rate yesterday's effort 1–10; no judgment, just data.

28.principle: Same move, different place — proof the habit is really yours.
28.science: Context shifts reveal how solid the pattern really is (generalization).
28.action: Try the same micro-action somewhere unfamiliar but safe.

29.principle: Big rewards can dull inner drive — keep them measured.
29.science: Heavy external reward can weaken intrinsic motivation (Lepper).
29.action: Make the reward small and immediate: done? mark it.

30.principle: Day 30: the question isn't 'did you?' but 'which version am I on?'
30.science: When identity language aligns with behavior, execution gets easier (self-perception).
30.action: Do the same mini variation a third time today, using your habit name.

31.principle: Stress isn't a free pass — shrink the version and keep showing up.
31.science: Stress disrupts autopilot; a planned micro version helps you recover (coping).
31.action: If stress is 7+, do half the version — a check-in still counts.

32.principle: A missed day isn't failure; it's a recovery window.
32.science: Brief review plus a small plan makes the next try predictable (Dweck).
32.action: If you missed, write one line: tomorrow's 30-second plan — not the reason why.

33.principle: Your mind sells delay as 'no pain right now' — tie yourself to something small.
33.science: Instant reward makes the future look smaller; commitment devices help (Mischel).
33.action: Leave a mini commitment with someone or somewhere visible.

34.principle: Someone asking 'did you do it?' goes beyond a reminder.
34.action: Ask one person today for a one-line 'will you check on me?'

35.principle: Resistance is like a muscle — skip rest and you tear a hole.
35.science: Self-control is finite; sleep and burnout block you (Galliot).
35.action: On burnout signals, drop to 10 seconds or one rep — sleep comes first.

36.principle: How your inner voice talks decides whether you act.
36.action: Calm the inner voice in third person, then take one action.

37.principle: Progress needs perception, measurement, and consistency together.
37.science: Pace picks up near the finish — small flags build momentum (Hull).
37.action: Plant a flag: I'm at 37/44 reinforcement; today's one rep.

38.principle: Inner conflict shrinks when you move, even a little.
38.science: Inconsistency feels uncomfortable; one step restores alignment (Festinger).
38.action: Say: 'The me who did this will try that once too.'

39.principle: A hard day still counts at 10 seconds — don't snap the chain.
39.science: The smallest rep after a break keeps the path alive (disruption psychology).
39.action: Light day: one action, no more than 10 seconds.

40.principle: When the setting fights you, it's a cue change — not punishment.
40.science: New cues produce new responses; adjusting environment works (behavior analysis).
40.action: List one bad environment variable; pick one to fix tomorrow.

41.principle: Inner reward isn't handed out; you learn it each completion.
41.science: Intrinsic reinforcement carries long-term motivation (Deci & Ryan).
41.action: When done, say inwardly: 'This is a small moment that defines me.'

42.principle: Postponing and quitting are different pockets — don't mix them.
42.science: Seeing the gap between intention and action opens room to intervene (Prochaska).
42.action: If you delayed, don't say 'I quit'; cap the time and return to mini.

43.principle: Final reinforcement week: your bottleneck is your anchor — rename it.
43.science: Automation takes 18–254 days; 66 is average, yours may differ (Lally).
43.action: Give your most-missed trigger a new one-line name — keep the trigger itself.

44.principle: Phase two done: your identity line now ties to action — automation next.
44.science: As habits automate, conscious load drops (Wood).
44.action: In 10 seconds, answer: 'Where do I do this without noticing?'

45.principle: Day 45: you're not deciding anymore — you're remembering.
45.science: Automatic execution shifts to basal ganglia; prefrontal load eases (Willingham).
45.action: At the trigger, notice silently for 2 seconds — no judgment.

46.principle: Autopilot isn't guilt — it's bandwidth you earned.
46.science: Automation frees cognitive load for bigger goals (dual-process).
46.action: Say today: 'I'm letting this run' — celebrate, don't blame.

47.principle: 66 days is an average; your pace being different is normal.
47.science: Individual ranges spread widely, often 18–66+ days (Lally).
47.action: Log feel, not score today: mindlessness/flow from 1–5.

48.principle: Boredom isn't missing discipline; it's the system humming.
48.science: Routine is less dopamine spike, more steady repetition (habituation).
48.action: When bored, tell yourself: 'Boredom means I'm entering automation.'

49.principle: Identity is measured by small moves you won't forget.
49.action: Say aloud for 5 seconds, to mirror or someone: 'I'm the one who does this.'

50.principle: Redesigning isn't stopping; it's carrying the same core forward.
50.science: Context shifts loosen habits — that's a chance to re-anchor (habit-discontinuity).
50.action: Test one context variable: different time, same micro-action.

51.principle: Trusting automation isn't dropping control — it's carrying a backup.
51.action: Write one backup: 'If I miss the log, same day, 30 seconds, same anchor.'

52.principle: A recovery day isn't a failed day; it's the system signaling.
52.science: Relapse prevention means naming trigger and mood, then shrinking the plan (Marlatt).
52.action: If a slip is likely: trigger + feeling in one line; fix with mini if-then.

53.principle: Discipline isn't punishment; it's placing a small reward at the right time.
53.action: No big reward: after finishing, 10 seconds of calm movement (stretch, etc.).

54.principle: You fill the 66; the 66 doesn't fence you in.
54.science: After the goal, a new threshold or stacked habit sustains momentum (habit stacking).
54.action: Draft one line for 67+: 'Same anchor, plus 2 minutes up.' — write only today.

55.principle: Day 55: if you're slower than average, a slow journey still counts.
55.science: Personal variation isn't unusual — it's expected spread (Lally).
55.action: If it feels heavy, say 'I'm on a slow path' — action still under 2 minutes.

56.principle: Automatic doesn't mean asleep — it's unconscious execution.
56.science: Procedural learning often consolidates without you noticing (Willingham).
56.action: After the action, 3 silent seconds: 'What happened?' — no judgment.

57.principle: Context leakage can signal it's time to test a new anchor.
57.science: When settings change, old cues may return; planned re-anchoring closes the gap (renewal).
57.action: Design a tiny new anchor in 5 seconds — today is trial only, not full performance.

58.principle: The system carries you; rest your ego, align the pattern.
58.science: System 1 autopilot wins clashes; System 2 adjusts the environment (Kahneman).
58.action: Fix one permanent default in your space: light, door, or bag spot.

59.principle: Seven days to 66: logging isn't bragging — it's holding a mirror.
59.science: Self-monitoring doesn't blind execution; it makes it visible (Harkin).
59.action: Summarize your 1–5 feel trend since day 47 in one line — or write 'no data yet.'

60.principle: A habit isn't an ending; it's the key to the next door.
60.science: One habit can trigger the next — chaining makes it easier (Clear).
60.action: Add one link on top of your current chain: a 5-second 'after' plan.

61.principle: If community carried you, choose consciously for day 67: open or limited.
61.science: Social comparison boosts external drive; it can nudge intrinsic motivation (SDT).
61.action: Share or keep private — mark which feels more sustainable today.

62.principle: Final five: rhythm, not mood — the finish line carries the beat.
62.science: Momentum rises near goals, but burnout risk does too (goal-gradient).
62.action: Today: usual time, usual place — minimum variation, maximum loyalty.

63.principle: Day 63: you win by simplifying, not forgetting.
63.science: Extra planning adds cognitive load in automation; minimalism speeds execution.
63.action: Quietly remove one plan item — continue as if nothing changed.

64.principle: The finish isn't a victory lap; it's keeping a promise you made.
64.science: A final if-then near the end cuts last-minute slip risk (Gollwitzer).
64.action: Make one three-day promise with a witness — person or note.

65.principle: Day 65: don't postpone the next 66 — continue with the same core.
65.science: Continuity runs on expanding the core, not giant new leaps.
65.action: Same anchor + tomorrow just 1 minute — no variation, emphasis on continue.

66.principle: This isn't a race that ends — it's signing the contract you made with automation.
66.science: 66 is a symbolic bridge — automaticity varies by person, but ritual binds (Lally).
66.action: Mark your post-67 choice in one line: same / mini / grow — sign today.

1.title: When the brain is processing the new routine
1.body: In the first phase, the frontal shell is more vigilant: movement often requires conscious choice. The brain devotes “attention” to a new pattern; it's not fatigue, it's the price of learning. Small, finishable steps in the early days create less resistance than big plans.

1.title: Where does resistance come from?
1.body: The basal ganglia pattern is unclear when the habit is not yet automatic; that's why the body sometimes says “no.” Avoidance is normal. The goal is not zero avoidance — choosing the smallest return instead of guilt when a run is seen.

1.title: Small reward, big distance
1.body: In the early phase, the big reward comes infrequently; the brain may shift to short-term comfort. A small completed step increases the feeling of completion. Reduce today's gain to the body or one sentence — that's enough evidence for the long journey.

1.title: Sentence for establishment phase
1.body: In this phase, context and repeat wins: same row, same clue, same anchor. Your identity sentence should be short; you will give yourself "one vote" for each repetition. Discipline is the practice of reminding, not performance.

2.title: Intermediate period: boring but valuable
2.body: In the reinforcement phase, some movement becomes familiar; the boring feeling may increase. This isn't usually a weakness — it's a byproduct of automation. Boring days paint the map “colorless but steady.”

2.title: Context flexibility
2.body: When circumstances change, the brain looks for new clues. Being prepared for small environment changes (location, duration, sequence) is a savior in the days of decline. Start in “good enough” condition without waiting for the perfect condition.

2.title: Identity and repetition
2.body: In this phase, the sentence "I am someone who does this" becomes stronger as it is repeated. Attach the behavior to the person, but do not upload it like an exam — gentle repetition. If one day you are disrupted, the identity does not collapse with a single day; reconnecting is also part of the identity.

2.title: Sentence for reinforcement phase
2.body: Stability outlives inspiration. Motivation is wavy; routine is the wave-breaking scaffold. The one small move made today makes the rest of tomorrow more likely.

3.title: The gateway to automation
3.body: In the final phase, more repetition and context cohesion are in play; the brain's “I'll do it later” cost sometimes drops. This doesn't mean ’easy now‘ — it means getting closer to 'starting small‘.

3.title: Triggers and traps
3.body: Even the automated routine is shaken by stress or lack of sleep. Keeping known triggers in writing is protective: fatigue, congestion, social pressure. The plan is not highly exciting, let's get ready to fall.

3.title: Identity reinforcement
3.body: As you approach day 66, the goal isn't just to close a box — it's to align this behaviour with your sense of self. The more you feel 'I chose this', the more likely you are to keep going (close to subjective autonomy in SDT language).

3.title: A line for the automation phase
3.body: The goal is no longer a 'perfect day' but recovering quickly when you slip. The long road is the sum of small repairs. Even the smallest reconnection today carries you forward.

coachNotes.day7: One week done. The anchor is settling — each day feels a little easier.
coachNotes.day14: Two weeks behind you. New pathways are forming; progress continues even when you don't feel it.
coachNotes.day22: Critical threshold: most people who reach here finish. You're here.
coachNotes.day30: 30 days. The question isn't 'did you do it?' — it's 'who are you becoming?'
coachNotes.day44: Phase 2 complete. Most days no longer feel like a choice — more like flow.
coachNotes.day66: 66 days. This rhythm is yours now — no one can take what you built.

byDay.10: Day ten: repeated presence beats intensity.

Feelings fluctuate; your system and anchor remain.
One rep is sometimes enough to keep the chain alive.
Your brain is logging today too — mark it, even small.
Slow progress always beats standing still.
Self-compassion isn't the opposite of discipline — it's fuel.
A planned start beats random motivation.
Tweaking the environment wins without draining willpower.
Today ask 'how small can I go?' not 'how do I feel?'
Consistency matters more than intensity.
The old habit may still tempt you — that's normal; the new path is strengthening.
One deep breath + one rep can save the day.
Identity is built by small repeated moves, not big speeches.
A miss isn't the end — it's data for tomorrow.

Automation requires patience — boredom is part of progress.
Measure yourself, don't judge; data is your map.
Pace may rise as you approach the goal — protect your breath and rhythm.
One sentence of intention lightens the rest of the day.
This journey isn't 66 days — it's a habit re-chosen every day.
Showing up today is already a meaningful win.

permissionRationale.title: Allow notifications
permissionRationale.message:
Rito sends local reminders on your device only — nothing goes to our servers.

• Morning: your Today step at the time you choose
• Evening: gentle check-in before the day ends
• Journey: phase milestones on your 66-day path
• Tomorrow plan: reminder for your planned action

fiveSecondTrainer.scienceKicker: A clear trigger + short window makes action easier — each round rehearses the neural path.
fiveSecondTrainer.rule1: Start → stay ready until the countdown ends
fiveSecondTrainer.rule2: When GO! appears, actually do the habit move
fiveSecondTrainer.rule3: Tap “Done” when finished — speed earns bonus XP
fiveSecondTrainer.actionHint: Make one clear move now. Tap below when done — faster means more stars.
fiveSecondTrainer.growthText: Reps strengthen the path — think of each round as one training set.
fiveSecondTrainer.skip: Not in the mood to play

flavor.karar: One clear move — shorten the thinking gap.
flavor.baglam: The trigger can stay even when the setting shifts.
flavor.energi: A minimum dose still counts on low-energy days.
flavor.sosyal: Press your own start button without waiting for approval.

proactiveIntervention.title: Today looks like a risky day
proactiveIntervention.body: Automaticity may have dipped lately or consistency wobbled a bit. That's normal on a 66-day path — most people fluctuate here. A small intervention can help.

stackingModal.stackTitle: Now? Let's add a small layer on this identity.
stackingModal.sameHabitHint: Fresh round with the same identity goal; daily marks reset, muscle levels and notes stay.
stackingModal.shareMessage: I completed my 66-day journey: “{{habit}}”. It's now a layer of who I am. Building the next one on top. {{hashtag}}

celebrate.lead:

For 66 days your brain rehearsed this path — quiet construction. Today that structure is largely in place.

karar.title: Single-choice practice
karar.anchor: Each morning, first thing: write and do one 2-minute mini choice.
karar.reason: Low-friction reps for the decision-moment muscle.

karar.title: Breath before phone
karar.anchor: Three deep breaths before reaching for your phone — then pick one task.
karar.reason: Creates space between impulse and choice.

karar.title: Evening prep
karar.anchor: Before bed, pick one item for tomorrow (outfit or bag).

direnc.title: Five push-ups or squats
direnc.anchor: Before getting into bed, 5 reps — count and stop.
direnc.reason: Resistance muscle: a small physical 'start, don't debate' rep.

direnc.title: First five minutes
direnc.anchor: On the task you avoid, sit for 5 minutes only — you're allowed to stop when time's up.
direnc.reason: A time box to start — lowers pressure.

direnc.title: One dish / one email
direnc.anchor: Before leaving home, clear one plate/cup or reply to one email.

baglam.title: Context-switch pair
baglam.anchor: Once a week, do the same habit in a different place (balcony instead of living room).

baglam.title: Step-outside trigger
baglam.anchor: Right after taking off shoes, 2 minutes outside.
baglam.reason: A mini rhythm outside the home.

energi.title: Low-energy version
energi.anchor: When tired, do the 'mini version' (50% of normal time or intensity).
energi.reason: Energy muscle: keep going when conditions shift.

energi.title: Light and water
energi.anchor: On waking, open curtains + half a glass of water — then main habit.

sosyal.title: Shared micro-commitment
sosyal.anchor: Tell one person aloud: 'Today I'll do X for 5 minutes' — no reply needed.
sosyal.reason: Light social accountability, not toxic pressure.

sosyal.title: One round in noise
sosyal.anchor: In a busy office, 3 minutes: phone in drawer, one task.

exactAlarm.title: On-time reminders
exactAlarm.body: On Android 12+, morning and evening notifications need the "Alarms and reminders" permission to arrive on time. Without it, approximate reminders are used (more reliable when the app is open).
